fact: January 28 is exactly 28 days into the year, making it the only date where the day number matches how many days have passed!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for ice fishing because lakes and ponds often have their thickest, strongest ice layers right now.
Penguins in Antarctica are usually busy feeding their fluffy gray chicks around this time, regurgitating fishy meals that would make you gag but taste amazing to baby penguins.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around January 28 in most places, meaning you're gaining almost a quarter-hour more daylight each week.
Tree sap in cold regions is typically frozen solid right now, which is why maple syrup makers usually have to wait until March when it starts flowing again.
Hibernating bears are often at their skinniest right now, having burned through much of their body fat while snoozing for months without a single snack.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, January 28 falls during their hottest summer days when cicadas are often screaming so loud they can be heard from far away.
Snow crystals formed on January 28 are often more likely to be perfectly symmetrical because the super-cold, stable air creates ideal conditions for flawless ice geometry.
This date sits exactly 4 weeks into the new year, making it a natural checkpoint in the calendar.
Owls in North America are typically starting their mating season around now, which means forests often echo with hooting calls all night long.
The moon phase on January 28 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, you get some of the darkest winter nights perfect for stargazing.
